name: Push-Up
instructions: Start in a high plank position with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart.
Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels — no sagging hips.
Lower your chest toward the floor by bending your elbows at a 45° angle.
Stop just before your chest touches the ground.
Push through your palms to return to the starting position.
Breathe in on the way down, breathe out as you push up.
tips: If too hard, drop to your knees. Keep core tight throughout.

name: Bench Press
instructions: Lie flat on the bench with your feet firmly on the floor.
Grip the bar slightly wider than shoulder-width with an overhand grip.
Unrack the bar and hold it directly above your chest, arms fully extended.
Lower the bar slowly to your mid-chest, keeping elbows at ~75° from your body.
Pause briefly when the bar touches your chest.
Press the bar back up explosively to the starting position.
tips: Always use a spotter for heavy weights. Keep your back flat — avoid excessive arching.

name: Incline Bench Press
instructions: Set the bench to a 30–45° incline and sit back with feet flat on the floor.
Grip the barbell slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Unrack and hold the bar above your upper chest.
Lower the bar slowly to your upper chest, keeping elbows at about 75°.
Pause briefly at the bottom, then press back up powerfully.
Maintain a neutral wrist position throughout.
tips: A 30° angle targets the upper chest best. Too steep and it becomes more of a shoulder press.

name: Decline Bench Press
instructions: Set the bench to a 15–30° decline and secure your legs under the pad.
Grip the barbell slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Unrack and hold the bar above your lower chest.
Lower the bar to your lower chest in a controlled arc.
Press back up explosively to the starting position.
Re-rack carefully with a spotter's help.
tips: Use a spotter — the decline position makes it harder to bail safely.

name: Incline Dumbbell Press
instructions: Set bench to 30–45° incline, sit with a dumbbell in each hand on your thighs.
Kick the dumbbells up to shoulder level as you lie back.
Hold the dumbbells at chest level with palms facing forward.
Press both dumbbells upward until arms are fully extended.
Lower them slowly to the starting position.
Keep a slight bend in your elbows at the top — don't lock out.
tips: Dumbbells allow a greater range of motion than a barbell. Focus on squeezing at the top.

name: Dumbbell Flye
instructions: Lie flat on a bench holding a dumbbell in each hand above your chest, palms facing each other.
Keep a slight, fixed bend in your elbows throughout the movement.
Lower the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc until you feel a deep chest stretch.
Stop when your elbows are at shoulder level.
Squeeze your chest and bring the dumbbells back up to the starting position.
Imagine hugging a large tree trunk.
tips: This is an isolation move — keep the weight light and focus on the stretch and squeeze.

name: Cable Crossover
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the high position and stand in the center.
Grab one handle in each hand and step forward with one foot for balance.
Start with arms extended to the sides at shoulder height, slight elbow bend.
Pull the handles down and together in a wide arc, crossing them at your midline.
Squeeze your chest hard at the bottom for 1–2 seconds.
Slowly return to the starting position with control.
tips: You can adjust pulley height to target upper, mid, or lower chest. Avoid using momentum.

name: Chest Dip
instructions: Grip the parallel bars and support your body with arms fully extended.
Lean your torso forward at about 30° to emphasize the chest.
Lower your body by bending your elbows until your upper arms are parallel to the floor.
You should feel a deep stretch through your chest at the bottom.
Press back up by extending your elbows, maintaining the forward lean.
Avoid locking out elbows at the top.
tips: Stay leaning forward throughout. Upright posture shifts the work to triceps instead.

name: Pec Deck Machine
instructions: Sit on the machine with your back flat against the pad.
Place your forearms on the pads (or grip the handles) at chest height.
Keep a slight bend in your elbows throughout.
Bring both arms together in a wide arc, squeezing your chest at the center.
Hold the contraction for 1 second.
Slowly return to the starting position with full control.
tips: Great for beginners and as a finishing exercise. Focus on the mind-muscle connection.

name: Diamond Push-Up
instructions: Get into a push-up position and bring your hands together under your chest.
Form a diamond shape with your thumbs and index fingers.
Keep your elbows close to your torso throughout.
Lower your chest toward your hands, keeping your body rigid.
Push back up until arms are fully extended.
Keep core tight and hips level throughout.
tips: One of the best bodyweight moves for triceps. If too hard, elevate your hands on a bench.

name: Dumbbell Row
instructions: Place one knee and hand on a bench for support, holding a dumbbell in the opposite hand.
Let the dumbbell hang straight down with arm fully extended.
Pull the dumbbell up toward your hip by driving your elbow toward the ceiling.
Squeeze your back muscles at the top of the movement.
Lower the dumbbell slowly back to the starting position.
Complete all reps on one side before switching.
tips: Keep your back flat and parallel to the floor. Don't rotate your torso.

name: Lat Pulldown
instructions: Sit at the lat pulldown machine and secure your thighs under the pad.
Grip the bar wider than shoulder-width with an overhand grip.
Lean back slightly — about 30° — to create a natural pulling angle.
Pull the bar down to your upper chest by driving your elbows down and back.
Squeeze your lats at the bottom of the movement.
Slowly return the bar to the starting position with arms fully extended.
tips: Avoid pulling the bar behind your neck. Focus on using your back, not your arms.

name: Pull-Up
instructions: Hang from a pull-up bar with hands shoulder-width apart, overhand grip.
Engage your core and squeeze your shoulder blades down and back.
Pull your chest toward the bar by driving your elbows down.
Continue until your chin clears the bar.
Pause briefly at the top.
Lower yourself slowly to a full hang — don't rush the negative.
tips: The pull-up is one of the best upper body exercises. Use a band for assistance until you build strength.

name: Chin-Up
instructions: Hang from a bar with hands shoulder-width apart, palms facing toward you (supinated grip).
Engage your core and pull your shoulder blades down.
Drive your elbows toward your hips to pull yourself up.
Continue until your chin passes the bar.
Hold briefly at the top, squeezing your biceps and back.
Lower yourself slowly and with control.
tips: The underhand grip recruits more biceps compared to a standard pull-up. Great for arm size too.

name: Deadlift
instructions: Stand with the bar over your mid-foot, feet hip-width apart.
Hinge at the hips and grip the bar just outside your legs (double overhand).
Drop your hips, lift your chest, and create a flat (neutral) spine.
Take a big breath, brace your core hard, and push the floor away.
Keep the bar dragging up your shins and thighs.
Lock out at the top by squeezing your glutes — then hinge back down to reset.
tips: The king of all exercises. Start light and nail the form. Never round your lower back.

name: Bent-Over Barbell Row
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, holding a barbell with an overhand grip.
Hinge forward until your torso is nearly parallel to the floor.
Let the bar hang at arm's length, keeping your back flat.
Pull the bar up to your lower chest/upper abdomen by driving your elbows back.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the top.
Lower the bar slowly to the starting position.
tips: Keep your back neutral and core braced throughout. Avoid jerking the weight up.

name: Seated Cable Row
instructions: Sit at the cable row machine with feet flat on the footpads.
Grip the handle (V-bar or wide bar) and sit upright with a slight lean back.
Pull the handle toward your lower chest/upper abdomen.
Drive your elbows back and squeeze your shoulder blades at the end.
Hold the contraction for 1 second.
Slowly extend your arms and let your shoulder blades protract before the next rep.
tips: Don't use body momentum to pull. Sit tall throughout and let the back do the work.

name: T-Bar Row
instructions: Stand over the T-bar machine, feet shoulder-width apart, straddling the bar.
Hinge forward at the hips, keeping your back flat and parallel to the floor.
Grip the handles and let the weight hang at arm's length.
Pull the weight up toward your chest by driving your elbows back.
Squeeze your back muscles hard at the top.
Lower the weight slowly with full control.
tips: Great for building thickness in the mid-back. Keep chest up and avoid rounding.

name: Face Pull
muscle: Rear Deltoids, Upper Back, Rotator Cuff
instructions: Set a cable machine to upper-chest height with a rope attachment.
Grip the rope with both hands, palms facing each other, thumbs up.
Step back until your arms are fully extended toward the cable.
Pull the rope toward your face, flaring your elbows out and up.
Externally rotate your shoulders so your hands end up beside your ears.
Pause briefly, then return slowly to the start.
tips: One of the most important shoulder health exercises. Everyone should do these regularly.

name: Hyperextension (Back Extension)
instructions: Position yourself in the hyperextension machine with hips on the pad and feet secured.
Cross your arms over your chest or hold a weight plate to your chest.
Start with your torso hanging down toward the floor.
Raise your upper body by extending your hips until your body forms a straight line.
Squeeze your glutes and lower back at the top.
Lower slowly and repeat.
tips: Don't hyperextend past a straight line — it places excessive stress on your lumbar spine.

name: Straight-Arm Pulldown
instructions: Stand facing a high cable pulley with a straight bar or rope attachment.
Grip the bar slightly wider than shoulder-width, arms extended in front.
Hinge slightly at the hips and keep a slight bend in your elbows.
Pull the bar down in an arc to your thighs by squeezing your lats.
Keep your arms straight — this is purely a lat movement.
Slowly return the bar to the starting position.
tips: Focus on initiating the movement with your lats, not your arms or shoulders.

name: Barbell Squat
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointed slightly outward.
Place the barbell on your upper traps (high bar) or rear deltoids (low bar).
Take a deep breath into your belly and brace your core.
Push your knees out in line with your toes as you lower down.
Squat until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor.
Drive through your heels to stand back up, keeping chest tall.
tips: Start with bodyweight squats to master the form before adding weight.

name: Leg Press
instructions: Sit in the leg press machine with your back flat against the pad.
Place feet shoulder-width apart on the platform, toes slightly outward.
Release the safety handles and lower the platform toward your chest.
Stop when your knees reach about 90° — don't let your lower back lift off.
Press the platform away by extending your legs, but don't lock out your knees.
Control the descent on every rep.
tips: Higher foot placement targets glutes more. Lower placement focuses on quads.

name: Walking Lunge
instructions: Stand upright with feet together, hands at your sides or on your hips.
Step forward with your right foot, taking a long stride.
Lower your back knee toward the floor — stop just before it touches.
Your front thigh should be parallel to the floor, knee over ankle.
Drive off your front heel to bring your back foot forward to meet it.
Alternate legs with each step forward.
tips: Keep your torso upright throughout. Don't let your front knee cave inward.

name: Romanian Deadlift
instructions: Stand holding a barbell (or dumbbells) at hip level, feet hip-width apart.
Keep a soft bend in your knees — they should not move much.
Hinge at the hips and lower the bar down your legs, pushing your hips back.
Keep the bar close to your body throughout.
Lower until you feel a strong stretch in your hamstrings (about mid-shin).
Drive your hips forward to return to the starting position.
tips: This is a hip hinge, not a squat. The movement comes from your hips, not your knees.

name: Leg Curl (Lying)
instructions: Lie face down on the leg curl machine with your ankles under the pad.
Keep your hips flat on the bench throughout the movement.
Curl your heels up toward your glutes as far as possible.
Squeeze your hamstrings hard at the top.
Lower slowly back to the starting position.
Avoid raising your hips off the pad.
tips: Control the lowering phase — that's where much of the hamstring growth happens.

name: Leg Extension
instructions: Sit in the leg extension machine with your back flat against the pad.
Position the ankle pad just above your feet.
Extend your legs upward by contracting your quads until fully straight.
Hold at the top for 1 second, squeezing your quads.
Lower slowly with control — don't let the weight drop.
Repeat for the target reps.
tips: Best used as an isolation finisher after compound leg work. Avoid locking knees aggressively.

name: Bulgarian Split Squat
instructions: Stand facing away from a bench. Reach back and rest one foot on the bench.
Hop your front foot forward so your torso stays upright when you squat down.
Lower your back knee toward the floor by bending your front knee.
Keep your front knee tracking over your toes.
Drive through your front heel to return to the starting position.
Complete all reps on one leg before switching.
tips: One of the most effective single-leg exercises. Dumbbells or bodyweight both work well.

name: Goblet Squat
instructions: Hold a dumbbell or kettlebell vertically at your chest with both hands.
Stand with feet slightly wider than shoulder-width, toes pointed out 30–45°.
Brace your core and sit down into a deep squat, keeping your chest tall.
Use your elbows to push your knees out at the bottom.
Drive through your heels to stand back up.
The weight acts as a counterbalance — great for squat depth.
tips: Perfect for beginners learning to squat. Great warm-up for any leg day.

name: Sumo Squat
instructions: Stand with feet wider than shoulder-width and toes turned out significantly.
Hold a dumbbell or kettlebell in both hands hanging between your legs.
Brace your core and sit down between your feet.
Keep your chest tall and knees tracking over your toes.
Squat until thighs are parallel (or lower if mobility allows).
Drive through your heels to return to the starting position.
tips: The wide stance shifts emphasis to the inner thighs and glutes compared to a regular squat.

name: Hip Thrust
instructions: Sit on the floor with your upper back against a bench and a barbell over your hips.
Bend your knees so feet are flat on the floor, shoulder-width apart.
Roll the bar into position over your hip crease and use a pad for comfort.
Drive through your heels to thrust your hips up until your body is parallel to the floor.
Squeeze your glutes hard at the top — your shins should be vertical.
Lower your hips back down slowly and repeat.
tips: The single best exercise for glute development. Focus on squeezing at the top, not just lifting.

name: Standing Calf Raise
instructions: Stand on the edge of a step or calf raise platform with heels hanging off.
Hold dumbbells at your sides or use a machine for resistance.
Start with heels below the platform level for a full stretch.
Rise up onto your tiptoes as high as possible.
Hold the top position for 1 second.
Lower slowly to the stretch position and repeat.
tips: Calves respond well to high reps and slow tempos. Don't rush through these.

name: Step-Up
instructions: Stand facing a sturdy bench or box holding dumbbells at your sides.
Step up with your right foot, pressing through your heel to lift your body up.
Bring your left foot up to meet it on the box.
Step back down with your right foot, then the left.
Alternate the leading leg each rep, or complete all reps on one side.
Keep your torso upright throughout.
tips: Use a knee-height box for best results. Great for building functional leg strength.

name: Overhead Press
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, gripping the bar just outside shoulder-width.
Hold the bar at collarbone height with elbows slightly forward.
Brace your core and squeeze your glutes before pressing.
Press the bar straight overhead until arms are fully extended.
Tuck your chin slightly to allow the bar to pass your face.
Lower the bar back to the starting position in a controlled manner.
tips: Avoid leaning back excessively. If you need to lean back a lot, the weight is too heavy.

name: Lateral Raise
instructions: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides.
Keep a slight bend in your elbows throughout the movement.
Raise both arms out to the sides until they are parallel to the floor.
Lead with your elbows, not your wrists — imagine pouring water from a jug.
Pause briefly at the top.
Lower the dumbbells slowly back to your sides.
tips: Use lighter weight and focus on the burn. Avoid swinging or using momentum.

name: Arnold Press
instructions: Sit on a bench with a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height, palms facing you.
Press the dumbbells up while rotating your palms forward.
Fully extend your arms overhead — palms now facing away from you.
Reverse the motion: lower the dumbbells while rotating palms back toward you.
Return to the starting position with palms facing you.
The rotation hits all three deltoid heads in one movement.
tips: Named after Arnold Schwarzenegger. Go slow and controlled to feel the full range.

name: Front Raise
instructions: Stand holding a dumbbell in each hand in front of your thighs, palms facing you.
Keep a slight bend in your elbows throughout.
Raise one (or both) arms straight in front of you to shoulder height.
Hold briefly at the top.
Lower slowly back to the starting position.
Alternate arms or do both simultaneously.
tips: Avoid leaning back or using body momentum. Keep the movement strict.

name: Rear Delt Fly
instructions: Sit on the edge of a bench and hinge forward until your chest is near your thighs.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand hanging below your body, palms facing each other.
Raise both arms out to the sides in a wide arc, keeping a slight elbow bend.
Squeeze your rear delts and upper back at the top.
Lower the dumbbells slowly back to the starting position.
You can also do this on a pec deck machine in reverse.
tips: These are commonly underdeveloped. Strong rear delts improve posture and shoulder health.

name: Upright Row
instructions: Stand holding a barbell or dumbbells in front of your thighs, overhand grip.
Pull the weight straight up along your body, leading with your elbows.
Raise until the bar is at chin level and elbows are above your hands.
Your elbows should flare outward and be above your wrists at the top.
Hold briefly, then lower slowly.
Keep the bar close to your body throughout.
tips: Use a wider grip to reduce shoulder impingement risk. Stop at chest height if you feel discomfort.

name: Cable Lateral Raise
instructions: Stand sideways to a cable machine with the pulley set to the lowest position.
Grab the handle with the hand furthest from the machine.
Keep a slight bend in your elbow.
Raise your arm out to the side until it's parallel to the floor.
Hold briefly at the top, then lower slowly.
The cable provides constant tension throughout — unlike dumbbells.
tips: The cable keeps tension at the bottom of the movement where dumbbells lack it. Great for shoulder isolation.

name: Machine Shoulder Press
instructions: Adjust the seat so the handles are at shoulder height.
Sit with your back flat against the pad and grab the handles.
Press the handles straight overhead until arms are nearly fully extended.
Hold briefly at the top.
Lower the handles slowly back to shoulder height.
Keep core braced and avoid arching your back.
tips: Great for beginners or when you want to go heavy without balancing the weight yourself.

name: Dumbbell Bicep Curl
instructions: Stand holding a dumbbell in each hand, arms fully extended at your sides.
Keep your elbows tucked against your torso throughout the movement.
Curl the dumbbells up toward your shoulders by contracting your biceps.
Rotate your palms to face your shoulders at the top (supinate).
Squeeze your biceps hard at the top for 1 second.
Lower the weights slowly back to the starting position.
tips: Avoid swinging your body. Control the negative (lowering) phase for more gains.

name: Barbell Curl
instructions: Stand holding a barbell with an underhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart.
Let the bar hang at arm's length in front of your thighs.
Keep your elbows pinned at your sides and curl the bar up toward your shoulders.
Squeeze your biceps at the top — the bar should be near your chin.
Lower the bar slowly in a controlled arc.
Keep your upper arms completely still throughout.
tips: The barbell allows heavier loads than dumbbells. Use an EZ-bar if you have wrist discomfort.

name: Hammer Curl
instructions: Stand holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip (palms facing each other).
Keep your elbows tucked at your sides throughout.
Curl the dumbbells up toward your shoulders without rotating your wrists.
Hold briefly at the top.
Lower slowly back to the starting position.
Alternate arms or curl both simultaneously.
tips: Hammer curls target the brachialis — a muscle that pushes your biceps up for a bigger peak.

name: Concentration Curl
instructions: Sit on a bench with legs spread and lean forward slightly.
Hold a dumbbell in one hand and rest the back of your upper arm against your inner thigh.
Let the dumbbell hang down with arm fully extended.
Curl the dumbbell up toward your shoulder, rotating your palm up.
Squeeze your bicep hard at the top.
Lower slowly, completing all reps before switching arms.
tips: The braced position eliminates cheating and maximizes bicep isolation. Go slow for best results.

name: Preacher Curl
instructions: Sit at a preacher curl bench and rest your upper arms on the pad.
Hold a barbell or dumbbells with an underhand grip.
Let your arms extend fully at the start.
Curl the weight up toward your chin by contracting your biceps.
Squeeze at the top, then lower slowly — going all the way back to full extension.
Avoid bouncing at the bottom.
tips: The preacher bench targets the lower portion of the biceps. Great for adding fullness.

name: Incline Dumbbell Curl
instructions: Set a bench to 60° incline and sit back with a dumbbell in each hand.
Let your arms hang straight down — the incline creates a stretch on the long head of the bicep.
Curl both dumbbells up toward your shoulders while keeping your upper arms stationary.
Squeeze at the top.
Lower slowly to the fully extended, stretched position.
Avoid swinging your arms forward.
tips: The stretch at the bottom is the key advantage of this exercise. Don't rush the lowering phase.

name: Cable Curl
instructions: Stand facing a low cable machine with a straight bar or EZ-bar attachment.
Grip the bar with an underhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart.
Keep your elbows at your sides and curl the bar up toward your chin.
Squeeze your biceps at the top.
Lower the bar slowly with full control.
The cable provides constant tension — unlike dumbbells.
tips: Cables are especially effective for the bicep because tension remains constant throughout the movement.

name: Tricep Pushdown
instructions: Stand at a cable machine with a rope or straight bar attachment at the top.
Grip the attachment and keep your elbows pinned at your sides.
Start with your forearms parallel to the floor.
Push the attachment straight down by extending your elbows fully.
Squeeze your triceps hard at the bottom.
Let the weight rise slowly back to the starting position.
tips: Keep your upper arms completely still. Only your forearms should move.

name: Skull Crusher
instructions: Lie flat on a bench holding an EZ-bar or dumbbells above your chest, arms extended.
Keeping your upper arms vertical, bend your elbows to lower the bar toward your forehead.
Stop just before the bar touches your forehead.
Extend your elbows to press the bar back up.
Keep your upper arms pointed straight up throughout.
Avoid flaring your elbows out to the sides.
tips: The name is the reminder — keep control of the weight. Great for hitting the long head of the tricep.

name: Overhead Tricep Extension
instructions: Stand or sit holding a dumbbell with both hands above your head.
Keep your upper arms close to your head, elbows pointing up.
Lower the dumbbell behind your head by bending your elbows.
Lower until your forearms are parallel to the floor or below.
Press back up by extending your elbows fully.
Keep your core tight and avoid arching your back.
tips: Overhead position maximally stretches the long head. Use a moderate weight and control every rep.

name: Close-Grip Bench Press
instructions: Lie flat on a bench and grip the barbell with hands shoulder-width apart (narrower than normal).
Unrack the bar and hold it above your lower chest.
Lower the bar to your chest with elbows tucked close to your body.
Press the bar back up, driving through your triceps.
Keep your wrists stacked over your elbows throughout.
Fully extend at the top without locking out aggressively.
tips: Hands too narrow can strain your wrists. Shoulder-width is the sweet spot for most people.

name: Tricep Dip
instructions: Sit on the edge of a bench with hands beside your hips gripping the edge.
Slide your hips off the bench and extend your legs out.
Lower your body by bending your elbows until your upper arms are parallel to the floor.
Keep your back close to the bench throughout.
Press through your palms to straighten your arms and lift back up.
For more tricep emphasis, keep legs straight; bent knees make it easier.
tips: Add weight on your lap as you get stronger. Keep your elbows pointing backward, not flaring out.

name: Cable Overhead Tricep Extension
instructions: Set a cable pulley to the highest position and attach a rope.
Face away from the machine, grab the rope behind your head with both hands.
Step forward to create tension, feet staggered for balance.
Keep your upper arms close to your head, elbows pointing forward.
Extend your elbows to press the rope forward and down.
Slowly return to the starting position with elbows bent.
tips: The cable provides consistent tension in the stretched position — a major advantage over dumbbells.

name: Plank
instructions: Start in a forearm plank: elbows directly under shoulders, forearms flat.
Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels.
Squeeze your glutes and brace your core like you're about to be punched.
Keep your hips level — don't let them sag or pike up.
Breathe steadily and hold for the target time.
Rest and repeat.
tips: Quality over time. A solid 30-second plank beats a sloppy 60-second one.

name: Crunch
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat on the floor.
Place your hands lightly behind your head — don't pull on your neck.
Engage your core and curl your shoulders off the floor.
Focus on bringing your ribcage toward your hips.
Hold the contracted position for 1 second.
Lower back down slowly without fully relaxing.
tips: Small movement done with full control beats a big movement done sloppily. Exhale on the way up.

name: Bicycle Crunch
instructions: Lie on your back with hands behind your head and legs in a tabletop position.
Lift your shoulders off the floor and engage your core.
Bring your right elbow toward your left knee while extending your right leg.
Switch sides, bringing your left elbow toward your right knee.
Maintain a cycling motion with your legs.
Keep your lower back pressed to the floor.
tips: Research consistently ranks this as one of the top ab exercises. Focus on rotation, not just elbow to knee.

name: Lying Leg Raise
instructions: Lie flat on your back with legs straight and hands under your hips for support.
Engage your core and keep your lower back pressed down.
Raise both legs together until they're perpendicular to the floor.
Hold briefly at the top.
Lower your legs slowly — stop just before they touch the floor.
Repeat without letting your feet rest on the ground.
tips: If you feel it in your lower back, reduce range of motion or bend your knees slightly.

name: Russian Twist
instructions: Sit on the floor with knees bent and feet flat (or raised for more challenge).
Lean back slightly so your torso is at about 45° — feel your core engage.
Hold your hands together, a weight plate, or a medicine ball in front of you.
Rotate your torso to the right, touching the weight to the floor beside your hip.
Rotate to the left side and repeat.
Breathe out on each rotation.
tips: Add a dumbbell or medicine ball to increase difficulty. Keep your spine long — don't round your back.

name: Dead Bug
instructions: Lie on your back with arms pointing straight up and knees bent at 90° (tabletop).
Press your lower back firmly into the floor and keep it there throughout.
Slowly extend your right leg straight out while lowering your left arm overhead.
Stop just before touching the floor.
Return to the start position and repeat on the opposite side.
Breathe out as you extend, breathe in as you return.
tips: The key is keeping your lower back flat the entire time. If it lifts, reduce your range of motion.

name: Ab Wheel Rollout
instructions: Kneel on the floor and grip the ab wheel with both hands.
Start with the wheel directly below your shoulders.
Brace your core tightly and slowly roll the wheel forward.
Keep your back flat — don't let your hips drop or back arch.
Extend until your body is almost parallel to the floor.
Contract your abs to roll back to the starting position.
tips: One of the hardest ab exercises. Start with partial rollouts and build to full extension over time.

name: Mountain Climber
instructions: Start in a high plank position with arms straight, hands under shoulders.
Keep your hips level and core braced.
Drive your right knee toward your chest.
Quickly switch, extending the right leg back while pulling the left knee in.
Continue alternating legs in a running motion.
Keep your hips down — don't let them bounce up.
tips: Can be done slow (strength focus) or fast (cardio/conditioning). Both are effective.

name: Hanging Knee Raise
instructions: Hang from a pull-up bar with an overhand grip, arms fully extended.
Engage your core and avoid swinging.
Bend your knees and pull them up toward your chest.
Curl your pelvis slightly upward at the top to maximize ab contraction.
Hold briefly, then lower your legs slowly.
Avoid using momentum — keep movements controlled.
tips: Progress to straight leg raises once knee raises become easy. Hanging variations are excellent for core strength.

name: Side Plank
instructions: Lie on your side and prop yourself up on your forearm, elbow directly under your shoulder.
Stack your feet or place the top foot in front of the bottom.
Lift your hips off the ground, forming a straight line from head to feet.
Keep your core and glutes tight.
Hold for the target time.
Switch sides and repeat.
tips: Drop your hip slightly toward the floor and lift it back up to make it harder — known as a side plank dip.

name: Cable Crunch
instructions: Kneel in front of a high cable pulley with a rope attachment.
Hold the rope behind your head with both hands.
Start upright with core engaged.
Crunch down by rounding your spine — bring your elbows toward your knees.
Squeeze your abs hard at the bottom.
Slowly return to the upright position without relaxing completely.
tips: The cable provides resistance through the full range — one of the best weighted ab exercises.

name: Kettlebell Swing
instructions: Stand with feet slightly wider than shoulder-width with a kettlebell on the floor.
Hinge at the hips, grip the kettlebell with both hands, and hike it back between your legs.
Drive your hips forward explosively to swing the kettlebell up to chest height.
Let the kettlebell float momentarily at the top — arms should be parallel to the floor.
Let the kettlebell swing back down and hinge your hips back to absorb the momentum.
Repeat fluidly — it's all hips, not arms.
tips: The swing is a hip hinge, not a squat. Keep your back flat and generate power from your glutes and hamstrings.

name: Burpee
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart.
Squat down and place your hands on the floor in front of you.
Jump or step your feet back into a push-up position.
Perform one push-up (optional but recommended).
Jump or step your feet back toward your hands.
Explode upward into a jump, reaching your arms overhead.
tips: One of the most effective conditioning exercises. Scale by removing the push-up or the jump if needed.

name: Sumo Deadlift
instructions: Stand with feet very wide (1.5-2x shoulder width) and toes turned out 45°.
Grip the bar just inside your legs with an overhand or mixed grip.
Drop your hips, lift your chest, and ensure your spine is neutral.
Push the floor apart with your feet as you initiate the pull.
Keep the bar close to your body as it rises.
Lock out at the top by squeezing your glutes.
tips: The sumo stance shifts emphasis to the glutes and inner quads compared to the conventional deadlift.

name: Good Morning
instructions: Stand with a barbell on your upper back, feet shoulder-width apart.
Brace your core and keep a soft bend in your knees.
Hinge forward at the hips, pushing them backward.
Lower your torso until it's close to parallel to the floor.
Feel the stretch in your hamstrings at the bottom.
Drive your hips forward to return to standing.
tips: Start very light — the movement is deceptively difficult. Great for posterior chain strength.

name: Dumbbell Shrug
instructions: Stand holding a heavy dumbbell in each hand at your sides.
Let your arms hang straight down — shoulders relaxed.
Shrug your shoulders straight up toward your ears as high as possible.
Hold at the top for 1-2 seconds.
Lower your shoulders slowly and fully.
Repeat for the target reps.
tips: Don't roll your shoulders — that adds no benefit and risks injury. Straight up and down only.

name: Seated Alternating Curl
instructions: Sit on a bench with a dumbbell in each hand, arms hanging at your sides.
Keep your back straight and elbows at your sides.
Curl one dumbbell up toward the same shoulder, rotating your palm as you lift.
Squeeze at the top, then lower slowly.
Repeat on the opposite side, alternating each rep.
Keep upper arms stationary throughout.
tips: Seated position prevents body swing — maximizing bicep tension on every rep.

name: Reverse Curl
instructions: Stand holding a barbell with an overhand grip (palms facing down), hands shoulder-width apart.
Keep your elbows pinned at your sides.
Curl the bar up toward your shoulders, maintaining the overhand grip throughout.
Squeeze at the top.
Lower slowly back to the starting position.
Maintain a neutral wrist position — don't bend your wrists backward.
tips: Strengthens the brachialis and forearms — often neglected muscles that contribute to arm size.

name: Wrist Curl
instructions: Sit on a bench and rest your forearms on your thighs with palms facing up.
Hold a barbell or dumbbells with hands shoulder-width apart.
Lower the weight by extending your wrists as far as comfortable.
Curl your wrists upward as far as possible.
Hold briefly at the top.
Lower slowly and repeat.
tips: Often overlooked — strong forearms improve grip strength in every other exercise.

name: Glute Bridge
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat on the floor, hip-width apart.
Place your arms at your sides, palms down.
Drive through your heels to lift your hips off the floor.
Squeeze your glutes hard at the top — your body should form a straight line from knees to shoulders.
Hold for 1-2 seconds.
Lower back down and repeat.
tips: Add a barbell across your hips to make it a beginner hip thrust. Perfect for glute activation.

name: Reverse Lunge
instructions: Stand upright with feet together and hands at your sides or on your hips.
Step backward with your right foot, lowering your right knee toward the floor.
Your left thigh should be parallel to the floor, knee over ankle.
Keep your torso upright and core engaged.
Push through your left heel to return to standing.
Alternate legs or complete all reps on one side.
tips: Easier on the knees than forward lunges. Great for those with knee sensitivity.

name: Box Jump
instructions: Stand facing a sturdy box or platform, feet shoulder-width apart.
Bend your knees slightly and swing your arms back.
Explode upward, driving your arms forward and jumping onto the box.
Land softly with both feet on top, bending your knees to absorb impact.
Stand fully upright on the box, then step (not jump) back down.
Reset and repeat.
tips: Always step down — don't jump down. Start with a low box and increase height as confidence grows.

name: Seated Calf Raise
instructions: Sit in the seated calf raise machine with pads resting on your lower thighs.
Place the balls of your feet on the platform, heels hanging off.
Allow heels to drop as low as comfortable for a full stretch.
Press up onto your tiptoes as high as possible.
Hold the peak contraction for 1-2 seconds.
Lower slowly and repeat.
tips: The seated position emphasizes the soleus, which lies beneath the gastrocnemius. Train both for full calf development.
